top_requirements.requirement: 2-5 years of direct people/team management experience (leading engineers or owning delivery for a team)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: ~250 of 803 postings cite a specific years-of-management figure; most common bands are 2+ (69), 3+ (85) and 5+ (60) years, with a smaller senior/director cluster requiring 7-12+ years

top_requirements.requirement: Prior hands-on software engineering background / "strong technical" credibility as a prerequisite to leading the team, not necessarily current coding output
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 374/803 use the phrase "hands-on" (mostly as required background e.g. "hands-on experience," "hands-on technical leadership"); 163/803 use "strong technical"

top_requirements.requirement: Bachelor's degree in CS or equivalent (often "or equivalent practical experience")
Ownership of hiring/recruiting for the team as a standing duty
Cross-functional stakeholder management (product, design, other eng teams, sometimes GTM)
Coaching, mentorship, and career/performance development of reports
Cloud/infra fluency (AWS most common, then GCP/Azure/Kubernetes) as a domain-context requirement, especially at infra/platform-focused EM roles
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 343/803 mention AWS; 132/803 Kubernetes; 128/803 CI/CD; 80/803 GCP; 72/803 Azure

top_requirements.requirement: AI/ML product or platform context named in the role itself (many roles are EM for an AI feature/product team, not just AI as company buzzword)
Security/compliance awareness expected of the team's delivery (SOC2, data protection, etc.), more common in fintech/health postings
Architecture-level technical judgment (reviewing/steering system design, not implementing it)

top_responsibilities: Own end-to-end delivery for one team (roughly 4-10 engineers at base-EM scope, occasionally 12-25+ at senior/director scope): planning, prioritization, execution, and shipping against a roadmap set jointly with product/design.

Run the full people-management loop directly: 1:1s, continuous feedback, individual growth/career plans, performance reviews, and being the primary voice in hiring, interviewing, and closing candidates for the team.

Stay technically engaged as an evaluator and steward rather than an implementer — reviewing code and architectural proposals, weighing in on technical direction/roadmap, and preserving enough hands-on credibility to be a trusted technical voice, without owning production output.

Act as the team's cross-functional interface — represent engineering in product/design planning, translate business priorities into technical execution, and manage escalations from stakeholders or on-call/incidents that land on the manager's desk.

Shape team culture, process, and (especially at senior/director-scope roles) org design — including budget/headcount input, staffing decisions, and increasingly for senior roles, managing managers rather than only ICs.

In infra/security/compliance-heavy orgs, ensure the team's delivery meets operational and regulatory bars (reliability, SOC2/compliance, incident response quality) as a team-level outcome the EM is accountable for.

comp_signals.range_text: Engineering Manager: salary range $240,000 - $300,000; Senior Engineering Manager: $300,000 - $375,000
base pay range (CA/WA/NY/NJ/CT) $265,000 - $325,000; all other US states $236,000 - $296,000
Engineering Director base salary $300,000 - $367,000; Engineering Manager (Capital) $198,000 - $293,000
Engineering Manager, Linux Team: annual salary $170,000 - $275,000 (one tier) / $190,000 - $303,000 (another tier)
Software Engineering Manager, AI Observability & Evals Platform: annual salary range $200,000 - $250,000
Engineering Manager, Billing Platform: base salary $182,000 - $242,000; Operations Engineering Manager, Fleet Reliability: $143,000 - $191,000
Engineering Manager, Fern Docs: base salary $250,000 - $290,000; Head of Engineering: $300,000 - $350,000
Director of Engineering, Dynamic Platform: base salary estimate $220,000 - $300,000

seniority_distribution_notes: Heavily weighted to base/first-line EM: ~70% of the 803 titles are plain "Engineering Manager" (no seniority modifier), ~18% "Senior Engineering Manager," ~10% Director/Head of Engineering, and only ~1% VP-level. Lead/Staff/Principal-modified EM titles are rare (<1% combined). Years-of-management-experience asks cluster at 2-5 years (the dominant band), consistent with a first-line-manager-heavy sample, with a distinct smaller cluster requiring 7-12+ years that maps to the Director/Head postings. This confirms the brief's framing of EM as primarily a first-line, single-team role rather than a senior-management-dominated sample.
notable_divergence_from_existing_brief: Largely confirms the brief (team size 4-10 matches the "5-8 engineers" claim; hiring, 1:1s, coaching, and cross-functional stakeholder work are all heavily and consistently represented; comp figures found here, $143K-$375K base across 68 companies with stated ranges, are directionally consistent with the brief's comp-parity argument once total comp/equity is added on top of these base figures). The one place the sample nuances the brief rather than contradicts it: postings put much heavier and more explicit emphasis on the EM's own technical background and "hands-on" credibility than the brief's "day every 3-4 months" framing might suggest to a reader — 374/803 postings use "hands-on" and 464/803 mention "architecture," almost always as a stated requirement for the manager themselves (e.g., "hands-on technical leadership," "deep hands-on experience with cloud infrastructure," "strong technical background required"). This is compatible with the brief's "in the code, not writing code" distinction (background/credibility vs. current implementation output) but the postings sample skews more toward technically-hands-on infra/platform/security EM roles than the brief's more general management-track framing implies — worth flagging if the taxonomy's technical_breadth_depth target (currently 2) is felt to be on the low side for this archetype. No evidence found to contradict the brief's core claims about comp parity structure, career ladder, or reversibility to IC.

dimension_evidence.note: Postings describe well-scoped team/product ownership with roadmaps set jointly with product, not "define the problem yourself" mandates — consistent with a mid target.
Escalations, on-call handling (65/803 "on-call", 147/803 "incident"), and cross-functional stakeholder work (428/803) recur constantly alongside planned work, consistent with a high target.
On-call/incident language appears but almost always framed as escalation ownership or team accountability, not the manager personally paging/diagnosing — consistent with a low-moderate target.
No postings describe the EM as the one doing hands-on debugging/diagnosis; code review and architecture discussion appear, not diagnostic work — consistent with target=1.

464/803 mention architecture, almost always framed as reviewing/steering ('drive the architectural vision', 'review architectural proposals') rather than personally designing systems — consistent with evaluator-not-designer framing at target=2.

374/803 postings use "hands-on" and 163/803 "strong technical" as explicit requirements for the manager's own background/credibility, more prominent and frequent than the brief's framing suggests — this sample leans toward technically-hands-on infra/platform/security EM roles; worth a review of whether target=2 undersells how much technical depth postings actually screen for, even if it's credibility rather than day-to-day output.
Postings consistently frame the EM as accountable for team delivery outcomes (roadmap execution, reliability, security/compliance bar) rather than personal output — consistent with a high target.

428/803 mention "stakeholder" and 425/803 "cross-functional", consistently framed as recurring coordination with product/design/other eng teams — a structured, recurring posture matching a mid target, not the fluid external-facing register-shifting of higher-target archetypes.
Coaching/mentorship language (369/803 "coach", 224/803 "coaching") is consistently framed as private 1:1 development, not public teaching/content — consistent with a low target.
No postings frame the EM's scope in terms of a portfolio of external client accounts — reports/stakeholders are internal, consistent with not-applicable low target.

Not discussed in client/account-relationship terms in any sampled posting — consistent with low target, though ongoing 1:1 report relationships exist, this dimension isn't about that per its definition.
All comp language found (base salary/base pay ranges at 68 companies) describes base + presumed bonus/equity structure, never commission or variable-pay-dominant comp — consistent with target=1.

No postings ask the EM to own production implementation; code review/architecture participation is the ceiling described — consistent with low target, though see technical_breadth_depth note on how much technical background is still explicitly required.
No meaningful travel requirements found across the sample; postings describe office/remote/hybrid work with no embed/travel component — consistent with target=1.
Security/compliance appear often (373/803, 249/803) but framed as team delivery bars to meet, not the EM personally doing adversarial threat modeling — consistent with target=1.

No physical/hardware-constraint engineering content found in the sample (even hardware-adjacent EM postings like CoreWeave's "Hardware Engineering Manager" describe managing a team, not personally doing physical-constraint engineering) — consistent with target=1.

Strongly confirmed as the defining trait: hiring (485/803), coaching (369/803), performance management (103/803), and career development (153/803) are the most consistently repeated themes across the entire corpus — supports the max target and weight.
No postings describe public speaking/content responsibilities for this role — consistent with low target.

A non-trivial and growing slice of postings are explicitly EM-for-an-AI/ML-product-or-platform-team (516/803 mention 'ai', 149/803 'llm', 120/803 'machine learning', several roles like Affirm's 'Senior Engineering Manager, Machine Learning Platform' or Anysphere's 'Engineering Manager, ML' require the manager to have real ML domain fluency, not just manage generalist engineers) — target=1 may be understating this for the subset of postings where ML is the team's core domain, though it's plausible this varies too much by specific EM role to warrant raising the archetype-wide target.
No meaningful mobile-specific fluency requirements found in the sampled postings — consistent with target=1.
Data infra appears in some postings but not as a widespread pattern distinct from general cloud/infra fluency already captured elsewhere — consistent with a low target.

Cloud infra fluency is explicitly and heavily required in a meaningful subset of postings (343/803 mention AWS, 132/803 Kubernetes, plus roles are frequently infra/platform-specific like 'Engineering Manager, Cloud Infrastructure', 'Engineering Manager, Internal Developer Platform') — for infra-focused EM postings specifically this reads as a real screened requirement, not just background noise; target=1 may undersell this for a meaningful subset of postings, though it's plausible this is domain-specific rather than archetype-wide and belongs to a more specific archetype split if one exists.
